coredata.dc:title: Temperature-responsive polymer-tethered Zr-porphyrin MOFs encapsulated carbon dot nanohybrids with boosted visible-light photodegradation for organic contaminants in water
coredata.dc:creator: Xia, Zhinan
Shi, Bingfeng
Zhu, Wenjing
Lü, Changli
coredata.dc:description: Zirconium-based porphyrin metal–organic frameworks (MOFs) have attracted extensive attention in the photocatalysis because of their considerable specific surface area, powerful visible light efficiency and semiconductor properties. However, the rapid charge recombination and self-aggregation limit their applications. Here, porphyrin-based Zr-MOF (PCN-222) nanohybrids (CDs@PCN-222@PNIPAM) as an efficient hydrophilic photocatalyst were fabricated by encapsulating various carbon dots (CDs) into PCN-222 nanopores with surface wettability modification by catechol-terminated thermo-responsive PNIPAM having various chain lengths. Due to the electron collecting effect of the nitrogen-doped CDs (N-CDs) and their synergistic effect with PCN-222, the photocatalytic performance of the prepared nano-hybrid photocatalyst were significantly improved. Furthermore, the introduction of hydrophilic polymer with moderate molecular weight effectively enhanced the transfer efficiency of photo-generated charges and the surface wettability of photocatalyst, and improved the water dispersibility of catalyst so that effectively promotes the enrichment and catalytic conversion of water-soluble target pollutants around the active site of the photocatalyst. The removal efficiency of RhB and tetracycline (TC) under visible light irradiation reached almost 100 % and 90.93 % in just 20 min, respectively. In addition, polymer-functionalized Zr-MOF-based nanohybrids revealed an interesting temperature-response switching behavior for the photocatalysis. The study on the catalytic mechanism and degradation pathways of TC showed that the h+ plays a major role in the degradation process, and other reactive species are also involved in the further mineralization of pollutants. This work provides a new insight for the functional modification of MOFs and the design of high efficiency photocatalyst.

head.ce:title: Temperature-responsive polymer-tethered Zr-porphyrin MOFs encapsulated carbon dot nanohybrids with boosted visible-light photodegradation for organic contaminants in water

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Institute of Chemistry, Northeast Normal University, Changchun 130024, PR China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Institute of Chemistry, Northeast Normal University, Changchun 130024, PR China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Zirconium-based porphyrin metal–organic frameworks (MOFs) have attracted extensive attention in the photocatalysis because of their considerable specific surface area, powerful visible light efficiency and semiconductor properties. However, the rapid charge recombination and self-aggregation limit their applications. Here, porphyrin-based Zr-MOF (PCN-222) nanohybrids (CDs@PCN-222@PNIPAM) as an efficient hydrophilic photocatalyst were fabricated by encapsulating various carbon dots (CDs) into PCN-222 nanopores with surface wettability modification by catechol-terminated thermo-responsive PNIPAM having various chain lengths. Due to the electron collecting effect of the nitrogen-doped CDs (N-CDs) and their synergistic effect with PCN-222, the photocatalytic performance of the prepared nano-hybrid photocatalyst were significantly improved. Furthermore, the introduction of hydrophilic polymer with moderate molecular weight effectively enhanced the transfer efficiency of photo-generated charges and the surface wettability of photocatalyst, and improved the water dispersibility of catalyst so that effectively promotes the enrichment and catalytic conversion of water-soluble target pollutants around the active site of the photocatalyst. The removal efficiency of RhB and tetracycline (TC) under visible light irradiation reached almost 100 % and 90.93 % in just 20 min, respectively. In addition, polymer-functionalized Zr-MOF-based nanohybrids revealed an interesting temperature-response switching behavior for the photocatalysis. The study on the catalytic mechanism and degradation pathways of TC showed that the h+ plays a major role in the degradation process, and other reactive species are also involved in the further mineralization of pollutants. This work provides a new insight for the functional modification of MOFs and the design of high efficiency photocatalyst.

ce:section.ce:para: Water pollution is caused by the random discharge of pollutants, including dyes and drugs[1]. Organic dyes as toxic and carcinogenic substances are widely used in various industrial production, and 15% of the commercial dyes are discharged into the water system every year [2-4]. Rhodamine B (RhB) has a carcinogenic risk to the human body, and its use in food and cosmetics has been banned, but it is still widely present in the textile industry [5]. As antibiotics seep into people's lives through sewage, the potential of antibiotics to treat human and animal pathogens is significantly reduced [6]. Tetracycline has been identified as a target contaminant in antibiotics [7]. And tetracycline can develop into antibiotic-resistance pathogens and endanger human health [8]. Tetracycline is difficult to fully mineralize, and RhB is widely present in domestic sewage. They have become target pollutants and have been extensively studied [9]. Hence, the problem of water pollution urgently needs to be solved. There have been many reports in the literature on wastewater treatment, such as chemical coagulation[10], adsorption[11], photocatalysis[12], separation[13].

Due to the structural diversity and customization of metal–organic frameworks (MOFs), as well as their super-high surface area and crystal properties, their potential applications in photocatalysis have attracted more and more attention [14]. Porphyrins and its derivatives are potential materials for the construction of MOF due to their excellent photophysical properties, strong-light absorption and easy modification [15]. The porphyrin-based Zr-MOF (PCN-222) has been extensively researched in the field of catalysis for its large hexagonal channels, high surface area, excellent chemical and thermal stability, and wide range of ability to capture visible light [16-19]. However, its poor dispersity and limited photogenerated charge transfer capability, PCN-222 is usually synergistic with other materials [20]. For example, g-C3N4 [21], TiO2 [22], metal nanoparticles [23], organics [24] and CdS [25] are the common materials for co-catalysis with PCN-222. These guests usually use PCN-222 as a carrier to form a binary catalyst to improve the catalytic performance of PCN-222. However, these catalysts are still limited by self-aggregation and poor dispersion in solvents.

Carbon dots (CDs) are one of the common nanomaterials with a typical size of 2–10 nm [26]. It has distinctive chemical properties: (i) outstanding water-solubility[27], (ii) low toxicity[28] , (iii) biocompatibility and easy functionalization [29]. As an effective electron donor and acceptor, CDs can greatly solve the problem of recombination of photogenerated electrons and holes in MOF. Many catalysts based on the synergistic effect of CDs and MOFs have been prepared in recent years, such as C60@PCN-222[20], CQDs@ZIF-8[30], amine-CQDs@UiO-66[31], MIL-53(Fe)/CQDs/ MNPs[32] and Zn-MOF@GQD[33]. The amine-CQDs in the catalyst synthesized by Yang et al. are heteroatom doped carbon dots, which are better than non-heteroatom doped carbon dots in terms of performance. However, the stability and the capacity of the CDs in MOF should also be considered during the synthesis of the catalyst. The self-aggregation problem of the above-mentioned catalysts still exists, so further functionalization of the catalysts is a feasible choice. In addition, there are few reports on the degradation of pollutants by porphyrin based Zr-MOF photocatalysts with CDs synergistic catalysis.

There have been some reports on improving the wettability of catalysts, but few of them have stimuli-responsive functional materials [34-36]. The stimuli-responsive materials make possible the realization of innovative functional nanomaterials, among which flexible polymers have attracted much attention[37]. Since the poly (N-isopropylacrylamide) (PNIPAM) with heat-induced phase transition feature was reported in 1967, the temperature has been the most widely studied stimulus in the field of responsive polymers[38,39]. Most of these applications are particularly concerned with aqueous solubility of polymer at lower critical solution temperatures (LCST) and upper critical solution temperatures (UCST)[39,40]. The properties of temperature-sensitive PNIPAM enable the functionalized MOF to achieve controlled release of guest molecules [41]. Moreover, MOF can be endowed with the function of capturing water through thermal phase transition behavior of the PNIPAM [42]. Cohen et al. first reported ZIF-8 nanoparticles with self-assembled porous monolayer and free-standing multilayer films in core–shell mode [43]. And they subsequently functionalized MOF with catechol-bound chain-transfer agent (CTA) to prepare mixed granular films. The method provided a modular and generalizable route for fabricating materials with inherent porosity [44]. It has been reported that the wetting properties of catalysts can improve the efficiency of photo-generated charge transfer in solid–liquid interface [45]. In addition, due to the modification of the hydrophilic groups on the MOF surface, the hydrophilic and hydrophobic microenvironment around the catalytic active center of the MOF is regulated, which greatly improves the enrichment and catalytic conversion of water-soluble reactants. [46]. Therefore, it is significant to functionalize MOF with PNIPAM, endowing MOF with temperature-sensitive properties. Especially, the studies on polymer functionalized MOF as a photocatalytic switch remains to be explored.

Here, we prepared a high-efficiency ternary photocatalyst 2N-CDs@PCN-222@PNIPAM with a synergistic effect between heteroatom-doped carbon dots and porphyrin based Zr-MOF that high-efficiency charge transfer, and excellent wettability and temperature-responsive switching imparted by a temperature-sensitive polymer PNIPAM. As shown in Scheme 1 , different heteroatom-doped CDs were pre-synthesized and wrapped in the PCN-222 channel through electrostatic attraction to form heterojunction. The formation of heterojunction shortens the electron transport distance of the binary catalyst and improves the photo-generated charge transport efficiency. The thermo-sensitive polymer was successfully grafted to the active site Zr of PCN-222 by catechol terminal groups of PNIPAM. This hydrophilic surface engineering of PCN-222 with PNIPAM enhances the surface wettability and dispersion of the photocatalyst in water, which promotes the enrichment and transformation of the water pollutants around the active site of catalyst. The self-aggregation of photocatalysts was been greatly solved. In addition, the MOF bonded with polymer will form micelles when the temperature exceeds the low critical solution temperature, which makes the catalyst easy to be separated from the photocatalytic system. And 2N-CDs@PCN-222@PNIPAM2 can realize the reversible micelle recovery when the temperature drops to LCST, so that the switch of the catalytic reaction can be controlled. The designed photocatalyst has achieved satisfactory results in the degradation of dyes and tetracycline, and its thermo-sensitive property make it easy to separate from water. Besides, the effect of different polymers (PNIPAM, P(PEGMA) and PMMA) on the surface wettability and performance of the porphyrin based Zr-MOF photocatalyst was studied for the first time, which is a highlight of our work and has important guiding significance for future related research.
Three carbon dots including citric acid-derived, nitrogen-doped and sulfur-doped CDs (GQDs, N-CDs and S-CDs) were synthesized as given in the support information.

The synthesis of PCN-222 was referred to previous literatures[16,47]. ZrCl4 (0.1 g, 0.55 mmol) and H4TCPP (0.15 g, 0.19 mmol) were added in a 50 mL three-necked flask containing 10 mL DMF, then 0.5 mL H2O and 1.2 mL acetic acid were added. The homogeneous solution was heated in a 120 °C oil bath for 15 min, and cooled to ambient temperature afterwards. The products were centrifuged and then washed with ethanol and dried. The synthesis of the PCN-222(Fe) is the same as the above operation. The H4TCPP is replaced with Fe-TCPP (0.16 g, 0.19 mmol).

20 mg of the as-synthesized PCN-222 were dispersed in 10 mL ethanol under ultrasonic treatment, and then 20 μL of the as-prepared GQDs solution (20 mg/mL) were added into the PCN-222 solution and the mixed solution was stirred one day. After vaporizing the solvent, the remaining products were further dried. The sample is named as GQDs/PCN-222.

ZrCl4 (0.1 g, 0.55 mmol) and H4TCPP (0.15 g, 0.19 mmol) were added in a 50 mL three-necked flask of 10 mL DMF, then 0.5 mL H2O and 1.2 mL acetic acid were added. Different amounts (10 μL, 20 μL, 30 μL) solution of N-CDs (20 mg/mL) were added to the precursor. The homogeneous solution was heated in a 120 °C oil bath for 15 min, cooled to ambient temperature afterwards. The products were centrifuged and then washed with water and dried. GQDs@PCN-222 and S-CDs@PCN-222 were also synthesized in the same way. Three samples of N-CDs@PCN-222 were synthesized by adding different volumes (10 μL, 20 μL and 30 μL) of N-CDs (20 mg/mL) and named as 1 N-CDs@PCN-222, 2N-CDs@PCN-222 and 3N-CDs@PCN-222, respectively. The synthesis of 2N-CDs@PCN-222(Fe) is the same as the above operation.

Three different molecular weights of PNIPAM were synthesized via a reversible addition-fragmentation chain transfer (RAFT) polymerization (Scheme S1) [48]. NIPAM monomer (1.136 g, 10 mmol), DA-CTA (0.0105 g, 0.1 mmol) and AIBN (13.12 mg, 0.05 mmol) were added in a Schlenk flask with 8 mL THF. Then, the mixture solution went through three freeze–pumpthaw cycles and stirred in N2 at 70 °C for 12 h. The polymer (PNIPAM1) was refined three times with diethyl ether (100 mL) as precipitant and centrifuged to collect. Finally, three polymers with different molecular weights, named PNIPAM1, PNIPAM2 and PNIPAM3, were synthesized by adjusting the molar ratios of [NIPAM]: [DA-CTA]: [AIBN] to 100:1:0.5, 200:1:0.5 and 250:1:0.8, respectively. Preparation of P(PEGMA) and PMMA is provided in the supporting information. And the structure of the relevant polymers in this work is shown in Scheme S1.

PNIPAM2 (0.1 g) and 2N-CDs@PCN-222 (0.1 g) were dispersed in 10 mL DMF and stirred overnight. The resulting product was centrifuged and washed with water to obtain 2N-CDs@PCN-222@PNIPAM2. Besides, we synthesized 2N-CDs@PCN-222@PNIPAM1, 2N-CDs@PCN-222@PNIPAM3, 2N-CDs@PCN-222@P(PEGMA) and 2N-CDs@PCN-222@PMMA in the same way.

In the photocatalytic experiment, Rhodamine B (RhB) and tetracycline (TC) were used as the target pollutants for photodegradation. Before light irradiation, the catalyst (50 mg) was dispersed in 100 mL solution containing pollutant (20 mg/L) and stirred for 30 min in the dark to achieve an adsorption–desorption equilibrium. A 300 W UV lamp with a 420 filter as the light source. And 4.0 mL of the reaction solution was taken out with a needle within a given time, and the residual amounts of pollutants was detected by UV–vis spectrophotometer after the photocatalyst was removed. The degradation products of TC were analyzed by high performance liquid chromatography-mass spectrometry (LC-MS) system.

The morphology and microstructure of photocatalysts were obtained by SEM and TEM (Fig. 2). The SEM images show that 2N-CDs@PCN-222 is uniform in size and consists of a particle structure with an average diameter of 310 nm (Fig. 2a). Moreover, because the polymer was post-functionalized to PCN-222, the diameter of 2N-CDs@PCN-222@PNIPAM2 is almost constant (Fig. 2b). TEM images clearly show that the CDs are evenly distributed in the 2N-CDs@PCN-222 with about 0.22Nm of lattice spacing (Fig. 2c and e). Due to the electrostatic attraction, there are also trace N-CDs modified on the outer surface of PCN-222. When the 2N-CDs@PCN-222 was bonded with PNIPAM, an interesting thing happened. The surface of the MOF became highly ordered, with lattice-like lines appearing (Fig. 2d). It is inferred from this phenomenon that the polymer chain is spread along the pore structure of PCN-222. And the TEM image displays that the CDs are in the 2N-CDs@PCN-222@PNIPAM2, and the lattice spacing is about 0.223 nm (Fig. 2f).

The physical structure of the photocatalysts was analyzed by XRD (Fig. S4). The pure N-CDs exhibit a broad XRD diffraction peak located at about 23.5°, corresponding to the graphitic structure [50]. The diffraction peaks of other materials are matched with the simulated peaks of PCN-222, and there was no obvious diffraction peak of N-CDs due to their low loading content and uniform distribution in MOF channels. When PCN-222 was uniformly anchored by PNIPAM2 or PMMA polymers, the crystal morphology of MOF did not change, proving that the skeleton of MOF was not destroyed in the reaction process with the polymer.

The chemical structures of the prepared photocatalysts were analyzed by infrared spectroscopy (Fig. S5). For PCN-222, there are obvious stretching vibrations at 1648 cm−1 for C = O and 1408 cm−1 for C-O bonds. The peak at 483 cm−1 is ascribed to Zr-O bonds in the Zr-oxo clusters [22]. Moreover, the vibration peaks at 1599, 1080 and 810 cm−1 are ascribed to C = N, C = C, pyrrole ring and para-substituted benzene ring. For N-CDs, the peaks located at 1648 cm−1(I), 1546 cm−1(II) and 1197 cm−1 (III) belong to the amide bond absorption peaks. And the C-NH-C, C–H and –OH/–NH bonds are located at 1408, 2934 and 3475 cm−1. The peaks of the skeletal vibrations from the benzene rings are observed at 1408–1648 cm−1. In addition, C = O vibration, C = C stretching, C-O stretching and CH bending are observed at 1648, 1750, 1197 and 1080 cm−1, respectively [50]. For 2N-CDs@PCN-222, there are the peaks of pyrrole ring and p-substituted benzene ring belong to PCN-222, and the amide bond peaks of the N-CDs appeared at 1648 cm−1(I), 1546 cm−1(II) and 1197 cm−1 (III). The peak position of Zr-O bond in 2N-CDs@PCN-222 is offset by 6 cm−1, indicating that there might be some interactions between the PCN-222 and the N-CDs. As for 2N-CDs@PCN-222@PNIPAM2, the peak of Zr-O bonds was blue shifted by 10 cm−1 from that of 2N-CDs@PCN-222, proving the interaction between the PNIPAM2 and 2N-CDs@PCN-222 [51]. The above description demonstrates the successful introduction of N-CDs and PNIPAM into PCN-222.

The surface composition and chemical states of 2N-CDs@PCN-222 and 2N-CDs@PCN-222@PNIPAM2 samples were analyzed by XPS. XPS full spectra (Fig. S6a) indicate the presence of Zr, O, N and C elements on the surface of the 2N-CDs@PCN-222 with the atomic percentage of 1.72%, 19.24%, 2.02% and 77.02 %, respectively. In the C 1 s spectra, the binding energies of O-C = O, C-O, C-N and C–C functional groups correspond to 288.7, 286.1, 285.5 and 284.4 eV, respectively (Fig. S6b). It should be emphasized that these functional groups exist in both N-CDs and PCN-222, so it is difficult to distinguish the signal of N-CDs by the signal superposition of carbon species [32]. Fig. S6c shows the XPS spectrum of N 1 s, where the binding energies at 400.2, 398.7 and 397.8 eV are assigned to C-N-C, N-C3 and –NH bonds. These indicate the presence of N in the pyrrole ring of the CDs and MOF [50,52]. In addition, the Zr-O bond (530.1 eV) was also found at high resolution O 1 s spectrum (Fig. S6d) [53]. As displayed in Fig. S6e, the 2N-CDs@PCN-222 has two peaks, Zr 3d5/2 (182.7 eV) and Zr 3d3/2 (185 eV). The introduction of CDs, which were bound to PCN-222 by electrostatic attraction, did not change the Zr chemical state of MOF. XPS spectrum in Fig. 3 a indicates the presence of Zr, S, O, N and C elements on the surface of the 2N-CDs@PCN-222@PNIPAM2, and the atomic percentage were 0.68%, 0.05%, 18.61%, 2.21% and 78.45%. Compared with 2N-CDs@PCN-222, the content of C and N elements in 2N-CDs@PCN-222@PNIPAM2 goes up, and that of Zr decreases, indicating the successful bonding of PNIPAM. In addition, the signal intensity of Zr-O in the O 1 s of 2N-CDs@PCN-222@PNIPAM2 was significantly enhanced as compared with 2N-CDs@PCN-222, showing that the polymer was connected to Zr through the adjacent hydroxyl of catechol terminal group of PNIPAM (Fig. 3d). The new signal peaks of S 2p1/2 (164.5 eV) and S 2p3/2 (162.8 eV) from the PNIPAM also appeared in 2N-CDs@PCN-222@PNIPAM2 (Fig. 3e) [54].

The weightlessness of PCN-222 and 2N-CDs@PCN-222 was analyzed by thermogravimetric analysis and the doping content of N-CDs in 2N-CDs@PCN-222 was calculated to be 0.87 wt% (Fig. S7). And the grafting percentage of PNIPAM2 in 2N-CDs@PCN-222@PNIPAM2 was about 4.6 wt% based on the weight loss of 2N-CDs@PCN-222 and 2N-CDs@PCN-222@PNIPAM2. The N2 adsorption isotherm measurements for PCN-222, 2N-CDs@PCN-222 and 2N-CDs@PCN-222@PNIPAM at 77 K were conducted to investigate their pore structures (Fig. 4 ). PCN-222 has a typical type IV isothermal curve with a Brunauer-Emmett-Teller (BET) surface area of 2408 m2 g−1. The nitrogen adsorption capacity of PCN-222 increases steeply at the point of P/P0 = 0.3, indicating a mesoporous structure [16]. After encapsulating the N-CDs in PCN-222, the isotherm type remains unchanged. However, compared with PCN-222, the adsorption capacity of nitrogen by 2N-CDs@PCN-222 was significantly reduced with a BET surface area of 1497 m2 g−1. The nitrogen adsorption capacity of 2N-CDs@PCN-222 increases steeply at the point of P/P0 = 2.5, indicating that the addition of CDs reduces the void size of the catalyst. The BET surface area of 2N-CDs@PCN-222@PNIPAM2 modified by the flexible polymer PNIPAM2 was further reduced to 288 m2 g−1. It has been previously reported that the in-situ polymerization of PNIPAM blocked the MOF channels [55]. It's possible for the PNIPAM chains penetrate into the 3.7 nm channels of PCN-222, and that explains why the BET surface area of 2N-CDs@PCN-222@PNIPAM2 decreases. Presumably, the polymer blocked the pores of 2N-CDs@PCN-222[56]. The BET surface area of 2N-CDs@PCN-222@P(PEGMA) and 2N-CDs@PCN-222@PMMA are not much different from that of 2N-CDs@PCN-222@PNIPAM2 (Fig. S8).

Fig. 5 a-b shows the energy band characteristics and optical absorption of photocatalyst. The light absorption edges of PCN-222, GQDs@PCN-222, 2N-CDs@PCN-222 and S-CDs@PCN-222 are 708, 756, 779 and 748 nm, respectively. With the introduction of CDs, the capability of light capture was enhanced, and the absorption edges were extended to longer wavelength, implying that the developed photocatalyst could be catalyzed synergistically by CDs and MOF [57]. According to the empirical equation Eg = 1240/λ, the band gap energies of the four photocatalysts of PCN-222, GQDs@PCN-222, 2N-CDs@PCN-222 and S-CDs@PCN-222 are 1.75, 1.64, 1.59 and 1.66 eV. Fig. S9 shows the Mott − Schottky plots for PCN-222, GQDs@PCN-222, 2N-CDs@PCN-222 and S-CDs@PCN-222. And positions of the flat bands at ~−0.40 V vs NHE (i.e., −0.60 V vs Ag/AgCl), ~−0.31 V vs NHE (i.e., −0.51 V vs Ag/AgCl), ~−0.34 V vs NHE (i.e., −0.50 V vs Ag/AgCl) and ~−0.36 V vs NHE (i.e., −0.56 V vs Ag/AgCl) are determined by the intersection, respectively. Based on the data available, the valence bands of PCN-222, GQDs@PCN-222, 2N-CDs@PCN-222 and S-CDs@PCN-222 can be calculated as 1.35 eV [58], 1.33 eV, 1.25 eV and 1.30 eV respectively. It can be seen that 2N-CDs@PCN-222 has a narrower band gap energy, and its electrons are more easily excited under visible light in the photocatalysis.

Fig. 5c shows the transient photocurrent (TPC) curves of the prepared catalysts over five on–off light progressions. Compared with pure PCN-222, the photocurrent signal of the photocatalyst doped with CDs is significantly enhanced, indicating that the combination of CDs and MOF can promote the photogenerated charge separation and transfer efficiency. In addition, PCN-222 was modified with N-CDs exhibited the highest photocurrent signal. However, the photocurrent signal of S-CDs@PCN-222 was declined when illuminated, and then gradually leveled off. Therefore, some photo-generated charges and holes in S-CDs@PCN-222 recombine quickly. From the EIS Nyquist plots (Fig. 5d), it can be seen that 2N-CDs@PCN-222 has the smallest semicircle radius corresponds, which further proves that it has better charge transfer and conduction properties.

The steady-state photoluminescence (PL) spectra were obtained at 420 nm excitation in order to study the photogenerated charge property of the photocatalyst (Fig. S10a). The PL intensities of 2N-CDs@PCN-222 is lower than that of N-CDs and PCN-222, indicating that the combination of N-CDs and MOF caused charge transfer between them under the excitation of visible light. Thus, the synergy between N-CDs and MOF improved the efficiency of photogenerated electron separation of the photocatalyst [59]. Compared with PCN-222, the PL intensity of S-CDs@PCN-222 is not significantly reduced, indicating that the charge transfer between N-CDs and MOF is weak (Fig. S10b).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Photocatalytic degradation of dyes and TC
ce:section.ce:para: Taking RhB as an example, we performed control experiments in visible light exposure to evaluate the performance of the synthesized photocatalysts. Herein, three kinds of CDs (GQDs, N-CDs and S-CDs) were synthesized to modify PCN-222, and the effect of CDs on the photocatalytic performance of MOF was explored. As shown in Fig. 6 a, compared with pure PCN-222, the GQDs/PCN-222 photocatalyst post-modified with GQDs increased the degradation efficiency of RhB by 12% after 10 min of light exposure. The defect of GQDs/PCN-222 is that photogenerated electrons need to migrate to the surface of PCN-222 after generation and then to GQDs [59]. Seeking to further improve the efficiency of photoelectric charge transfer, we added carbon dots to the precursor solution of MOF to synthesize more efficient photocatalyst in an encapsulated way. As can be seen from Fig. 6b, the electrons generated by the Zr-oxo cluster quickly transferred to the CDs nearby in the GQDs@PCN-222, which improved the degradation efficiency of RhB. However, the degradation efficiency is still limited, possibly due to the influence of carbon dot structure. It is known that the heteroatomic doped carbon dots have narrow band gap energies [60]. The synthetic N-CDs were encapsulated in MOF, and the effect of the resulted photocatalyst N-CDs@PCN-222 with different contents of N-CDs on pollutant removal was compared. The removal of RhB in water was 96.62% when 2N-CDs@PCN-222 was used as photocatalyst and exposed to visible light for 10 min, which is better than most reported contaminant removal studies (Table S2). This gratifying result should be attributed to the more abundant functional groups on the N-CDs surface and the lower band gap energy of 2N-CDs@PCN-222. However, the addition of too many CDs in the 3N-CDs@PCN-222 will block the MOF channel and affect the contact between the dye and the catalyst. Since the atomic radius of N is similar to C, the carbon dots doped with heteroatoms are mostly doped with nitrogen atoms. The electronegativity of S (2.58) is close to C (2.55), and the atomic orbitals around them do not match [61]. Besides, the size of sulfur-doped carbon dots (4.6 nm) synthesized by hydrothermal method does not match with the pore size of MOF, which lead to the unsatisfactory photocatalytic ability of S-CDs modified on the outer surface of PCN-222 (Fig. S11). In the existing reports, most of the catalysts involved in PCN-222 have metal coordination in the porphyrin ring [62-65], so we also synthesized 2N-CDs@PCN-222(Fe) as a control for photocatalytic experiments (Fig. S12). However, it was found that the catalytic effect was not significantly improved, and there might be secondary pollution caused by metal ions to the water, so PCN-222(Fe) was not used in the subsequent experiments.

To break the temperature limit, we studied 2N-CDs@PCN-222@P(PEGMA) degraded RhB at 40 °C. And the photocatalytic performance of 2N-CDs@PCN-222@P(PEGMA) is not affected by temperature (Fig. S15), which proves the universal applicability of our design. In order to prove the universal applicability of the photocatalyst in dyes, photocatalytic degradation experiment on methyl orange (MO) was also carried out, and finally a high removal efficiency of MO also was achieved by using the designed photocatalyst (Fig. S16).

We also optimized the photocatalyst for tetracycline degradation, as shows in the support information (Fig. S17). The final optimized photocatalysts are basically the same as described before, and these catalysts were used to degrade tetracycline. The photocatalytic removal efficiency of TC using 2N-CDs@PCN-222 and 2N-CDs@PCN-222@PNIPAM2 as photocatalysts were 82.60 % and 90.9 % in 20 min (Fig. 9 a). They exhibited photocatalytic activity with a 4.4-fold and 4.8-fold higher than that of pure PCN-222. And the corresponding apparent rate constants of the two functionalized catalysts in the photocatalytic process are 0.0294 and 0.0713 min−1 (Fig. 9b). The tetracycline was gradually degraded into small molecules. The total organic carbon analyzer (TOC) determined the final mineralization rate of tetracycline (Fig. S18). The mineralization efficiency of 2N-CDs@PCN-222@PNIPAM2 for the photocatalytic degradation of TC are 87 % and 99 % at 30 min and 60 min, respectively. Above result indicated that 2N-CDs@PCN-222@PNIPAM2 had a better adaptability to dyes and TC. Therefore, the photocatalyst design in this work can greatly improve the problem of water pollution, and provides idea for the exploration of this type of photocatalyst. Similarly, the photodegradation of the 2N-CDs@PCN-222@PNIPAM2 in TC was also tested for the temperature sensitivity. As shown in Fig. S19, the phenomenon was consistent with that in RhB, indicating that thermo-responsive PNIPAM gives the photocatalyst temperature-switching characteristics. Compared with the previously reported modified MOF-based photocatalysts, 2N-CDs@PCN-222 and 2N-CDs@PCN-222@ PNIPAM2 showed better catalytic activity for degrading tetracycline (Table S3). The photocatalysts have the absorption in the light wavelength range from 200 to 800 nm, so we also degraded tetracycline under simulated sunlight (Fig. S20). The photocatalytic removal efficiency of TC using PCN-222, 2N-CDs@PCN-222 and 2N-CDs@PCN-222@PNIPAM2 as photocatalysts are 24%, 86.8 % and 94.9 % in 20 min. Compared with the degradation of tetracycline under the visible light, the photocatalytic performance of the photocatalysts are improved due to the widening of the illuminated area.

The adsorption capacity of the photocatalyst is related to the surface charge state [4,67]. In order to infer the photocatalytic mechanism, the surface charge potentials of the prepared photocatalysts were measured, and the adsorption conditions of anionic dye (MO), cationic dye (RhB) and neutral molecule (TC) (pH = 3.3–7.6) [68] on the photocatalysts were compared. As displayed in Fig. 10 a, N-CDs, PCN-222, 2N-CDs@PCN-222 and 2N-CDs@PCN-222@PNIPAM2 exhibited the zeta potentials of −2.4, +30.4, +8.6 and + 14.9 mV, respectively. The adsorption capacity of several catalysts for pollutants was investigated. The synthesized catalyst was stirred with the pollutants (100 mL, 20 mg/L) for 30 min in the dark. The pH of the solution at the equilibrium of adsorption was 7. When the solids were removed, the remaining pollutants were measured by UV–vis spectrophotometer and the adsorption amount of pollutant was calculated (Fig. 10b). The pore of the PCN-222 enables it to have certain adsorption capacity. The adsorption capacity for RhB, MO and TC is 16.1, 20.7 and 4.5 mg/g, respectively. The abundant negative charge from the functional groups of the N-CDs made the Zeta potential of the 2N-CDs@PCN-222 significantly lower than that of pure PCN-222. But on the contrary, the adsorption of the 2N-CDs@PCN-222 to pollutants was significantly enhanced. Although the PNIPAM functionalized MOF resulted in the reduction of the BET surface area and pore volume of 2N-CD@PCN-222@PNIPAM2, the improved wettability did not significantly reduce the adsorption of 2N-CD@PCN-222@PNIPAM2 to pollutants. The results show that due to the combined effects of electrostatic attraction, specific surface area and wettability, the performance of the photocatalyst must be considered comprehensively.

In order to confirm that the polymer did not fall off from the MOF during photocatalysis, 1H NMR analysis was performed on the catalysts used for 5 times. 10 mg of 2N-CDs@PCN-222@PNIPAM2 after five cycles was dispersed in 0.6 mL of d6 -DMSO, then 2 μL of HF (48% H2O) was added, and the mixture was converted into the emulsion by ultrasound [43]. Fig. S21 shows the 1H NMR spectra of TCPP, PNIPAM2 and 2N-CDs@PCN-222@PNIPAM2 after five cycles. The signal of polymer still existed in the photocatalyst after use, which proved that 2N-CDs@PCN-222@PNIPAM2 had good stability. In addition, we also performed five cycle experiments of degradation of RhB and TC (Fig. S22), and X-ray diffraction was utilized to characterize the final photocatalyst (Fig. S23). Finally, the results again show that the stability of the photocatalyst is satisfactory.

The reactive species formed in the process of photocatalytic degradation were explored by radical scavenging experiments under visible light irradiation. The singlet oxygen (1O2), hydroxyl radical (
OH), superoxide radical (
O2 −) and holes (h+) by using L-histidine, tert-butanol (TBA), p-quinone (p-BQ) and ethylene diamine tetraacetic acid (EDTA) as scavengers respectively [22,69,70]. Fig. 11 shows the addition of each scavenger reduced the degradation efficiency of pollutants in the photocatalytic degradation process. Degradation efficiency of RhB were reduced to 76%, 91%, 33% and 70% by adding L-histidine, TBA, EDTA and P-BQ, respectively. Similarly, the degradation efficiency of the TC was reduced to 66.1%, 83.3%, 30.1% and 63.8%, respectively. It can be seen that the photocatalytic degradation of these reactive species is in the following order: h+ >
O2 − > 1O2 >
OH. Therefore, these four reactive species are the reasons for the degradation of pollutants, among which h+ is the main source. These results indicated that the synergism of CDs and PCN-222 improved the redox capacity of photogenic charge carrier. Fig. 12 validated the formation of ROS by ESR measurements. There was no signal in the dark. However, the signals were found of
O2 − and 1O2 with DMPO and TEMP as the trapping agent after visible light irradiation. We did not measure the behavior of the hydroxyl radical, considering its minor contribution.

From the above analysis, we proposed the reaction mechanism of TC and RhB photocatalyzed by 2N-CD@PCN-222@PNIPAM2 (Fig. 13 ). 2N-CD@PCN-222@PNIPAM2 produced electrons and holes under visible light (Eq. (1)). The h+ and e- produced by PCN-222 were transferred to the surface of the carbon dots under the influence of the interface electric field. In addition, the h+ oxidized the pollutants enriched by hydrophilic polymers directly rather than reacting with water to produce
OH because 2N-CD@PCN-222@PNIPAM2 (+1.25 V vs. NHE) has a higher VB position than E° (
OH/OH−) (2.3 V vs. NHE) [71]. Photogenerated electrons from the carbon dots combined with oxygen to form
O2 − (Eq. (2)), which in turn produced 1O2 and
OH (Eqs. (3) and (4)). Besides,
OH and
O2 − combined to form 1O2 (Eq. (5)). After the above steps, several reactive species were finally effectively involved in the photocatalytic degradation (Eq. (6)).
(1) 2N-CD@PCN-222-PNIPAM2 + hν → e− + h+
(2) e− + O2 →
O2 −
(3) 2
O2 − + 2H+ → H2O2 + 1O2
(4) 2
O2 − + 2H+ → 2
OH + O2
(5)
OH +
O2 −→1O2 + OH−
(6) h+/
O2 −/1O2/
OH + pollutants → degraded products

It has been reported that the active sites for the degradation of TC have been predicted using frontier electron densities (FEDs) theory [70,72]. The order and the degree of difficulty for different bonds in TC being oxidized by h+,
O2 −,1O2 and
OH were analyzed. More than ten intermediates are listed in Table S4. LC-MS determined the degradation pathways of TC (Fig. S24-S25). Dealkylation and decarboxylation reactions occur first when the RhB is degraded [73]. However, the difficulty of the organic reaction involved in the degradation of TC determines that tetracycline is more difficult to fully mineralized [13]. The specific degradation pathway is illustrated in Scheme 2 . At first, TC(m/z = 445) was attacked by h+ through a ring-opening reaction to form the product P5(m/z = 378) [74]. At the same time, dealkylation and hydroxylation allow products P1(m/z = 463), P3(m/z = 401) and P6(m/z = 335) to be further formed. P2(m/z = 431), P8(m/z = 305), P9(m/z = 278) and P11(m/z = 224) were formed by the action of the h+ and 1O2/
OH/
O2 −, respectively. P6(m/z = 335) can also be produced from P2 via ring-opening reaction and dealkylation. Furthermore, P4 (m/z = 389), P10(m/z = 232), and P12(m/z = 210) formed because of the oxidation of 1O2/
OH/
O2 −. And P9 can also be formed from P4 through the breaking of bonds [75]. P7(m/z = 323) and P13(m/z = 151), on the other hand, were formed via ring-opening reaction after the h+ attack and by the decomposition of P10, respectively. Eventually all the intermediates were mineralized into small molecules such as NH4 +, CO2 and H2O.

In summary, we developed a distinctive synthesis strategy for thermo-responsive polymer functionalized 2N-CDs@PCN-222@PNIPAM2 organic–inorganic hybrid as a photocatalyst for the effective removal of pollutants in visible light exposure with interesting temperature-controlled photocatalytic behavior. The optimized catalytic performance was obtained from the ternary photocatalyst synthesized with 0.87 wt% N-CDs encapsulated by PCN-222 and temperature-sensitive PNIPAM (Mn ≈10 kD) grafted on the MOF surface. The synergistic effect between N-CDs, PCN-222 and PNIPAM enhanced photoelectric charge separation and transfer more efficiently, and further improved the removal efficiency of pollutants by producing more reactive species. The MOF-based photocatalyst modified with hydrophilic PNIPAM has excellent wettability and improved the water dispersibility of catalyst so that effectively promotes the enrichment and catalytic conversion of water-soluble target pollutants around the active site of the photocatalyst. This design solves the long-standing problem of catalyst deposition. The degradation efficiency of the 2N-CDs@PCN-222@PNIPAM2 to 100 mL of 20 mg/L RhB and 20 mg/L tetracycline under visible light irradiation reached almost 100 % and 90.93 % within 20 min. In addition, the properly designed photocatalyst has excellent adaptability and stability. This work provides a new idea for designing the high efficiency ternary synergistic organic–inorganic hybrid photocatalyst based on MOF, CDs and polymer for environmental remediation.
